question_text: The matrix $\left[ { \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { x } & { z } \\ { 0 } & { 2 } & { y } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} } \right]$ is diagonalizable when $( x , y , z )$ equals (A) (0,0,1) (B) (1,1,0) (C) $( { \sqrt { 2 } } , { \sqrt { 2 } } , 2 )$ (D) $( { \sqrt { 2 } } , { \sqrt { 2 } } , { \sqrt { 2 } } )$
solution:
To determine when the matrix \( A = \left[ { \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { x } & { z } \\ { 0 } & { 2 } & { y } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} } \right] \) is diagonalizable, we need to check if it has a complete set of linearly independent eigenvectors. A matrix is diagonalizable if and only if it has \( n \) linearly independent eigenvectors, where \( n \) is the size of the matrix.

First, we find the eigenvalues of \( A \) by solving the characteristic equation \( \det(A - \lambda I) = 0 \).

The matrix \( A - \lambda I \) is:
\[ A - \lambda I = \left[ { \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 - \lambda } & { x } & { z } \\ { 0 } & { 2 - \lambda } & { y } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 - \lambda } \end{array} } \right] \]

The determinant of this matrix is:
\[ \det(A - \lambda I) = (1 - \lambda) \left[ (2 - \lambda)(1 - \lambda) - 0 \right] = (1 - \lambda)^2 (2 - \lambda) \]

Setting the determinant equal to zero gives us the eigenvalues:
\[ (1 - \lambda)^2 (2 - \lambda) = 0 \]
\[ \lambda = 1 \text{ (with algebraic multiplicity 2)}, \lambda = 2 \text{ (with algebraic multiplicity 1)} \]

For the matrix to be diagonalizable, the geometric multiplicity of each eigenvalue must equal its algebraic multiplicity. The geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue is the dimension of its eigenspace.

1. **Eigenspace for \(\lambda = 1\):**
   We solve the system \((A - I)\mathbf{v} = 0\):
   \[ A - I = \left[ { \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { x } & { z } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { y } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} } \right] \]
   The system of equations is:
   \[ 0 \cdot v_1 + x v_2 + z v_3 = 0 \]
   \[ 0 \cdot v_1 + 1 \cdot v_2 + y \cdot v_3 = 0 \]
   \[ 0 \cdot v_1 + 0 \cdot v_2 + 0 \cdot v_3 = 0 \]
   This simplifies to:
   \[ x v_2 + z v_3 = 0 \]
   \[ v_2 + y v_3 = 0 \]
   From the second equation, we get \( v_2 = -y v_3 \). Substituting \( v_2 = -y v_3 \
